metadata.title: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
metadata.study_design: Experimental study examining metabolic signatures during fasting
metadata.demographics: 18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m2)

evidence.quote: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years participated; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m2)
we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively

methods_participants.label: Study Design
methods_participants.value: Randomized crossover design with two study occasions separated by minimum of 21 days

methods_participants.label: Metabolomics Analysis Method
methods_participants.value: Non-targeted gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry

narrative_results.statement: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%)
Insulin stimulation during 72h of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized
There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting
Endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting
The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12h fast, was reduced by ~45%, but this did not translate into lower glycogen content.
The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences
In obese participants, levels of tricarboxylic acid cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast

There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting, indicating that myocellular energetics were preserved and there were no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 reports participant count (9 obese men); pass_2 reports BMI range (32-40 kg/m2) - different data types
Pass 1 specifies two timepoints (60 and 270 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one timepoint (60 minutes); these represent different sampling protocols
Pass 1 specifies two time intervals (180-210 AND 300-330 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one interval (180-210 minutes). These represent different measurement protocols.
Pass 1 discusses plasma glucose during HEC in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese vs lean. These are completely different metabolites and findings.

Pass_1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese during fasting. Pass_2 discusses maltotriose (glycogenolysis metabolite) in lean during fasting. These are completely different metabolites and different comparisons - not the same fact.

Pass_1 states maltotriose was reduced in lean only to levels comparable to obese. Pass_2 states obese shows loss of metabolic adaptation. These describe opposite patterns: pass_1 implies lean had greater reduction, pass_2 implies obese lost adaptation capacity.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: During HEC, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean

obesity associated with increased leptin and decreased adiponectin

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Insulin levels decreased by at least 50% during 72 hours of fasting in both groups [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Insulin levels decreased by at least 50% during 72h of fasting in both groups [fasting reduces insulin]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: During hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: During HEC, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean [obese > lean]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%) [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%) [lean > obese]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucose levels during hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp were statistically significantly higher in obese during 72 hours of fasting compared to both 12 hours fast within obese and lean during 72 hours fast [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Acetylcarnitine in obese were lower during 12h of fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72h of fasting [obese < lean (12h); obese = lean (72h)]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Insulin-stimulation suppressed free fatty acid levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting [insulin effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Insulin-stimulation suppressed FFA levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting [insulin suppresses FFA; effect reduced during prolonged fasting]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma free fatty acid levels were approximately 3–4 fold higher during hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp during 72 hours of fasting compared to 12 hours of fasting [fasting duration effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Plasma FFA levels were approximately 3–4 fold higher during HEC during 72h of fasting compared to 12h of fasting [72h fasting > 12h fasting]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Non-insulin-stimulated plasma free fatty acid concentrations were higher in obese during an overnight fast, but lower during prolonged fasting [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Non-insulin-stimulated plasma FFA concentrations were higher in obese during an overnight fast, but lower during prolonged fasting [obese > lean (overnight); obese < lean (prolonged fasting)]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12 hours of fasting in obese participants, increased free fatty acid content in skeletal muscle from obese participants was not found at any time point [obese characteristic]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Did not find increased FFA content in skeletal muscle from obese participants at any time point despite higher lipolytic rate during 12h fasting [no difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Increased fat mass in obese was associated with higher levels of leptin and lower levels of adiponectin [obese characteristic]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Increased fat mass in obese was associated with higher levels of leptin and lower levels of adiponectin [obesity associated with increased leptin and decreased adiponectin]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in the obese participants [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in obese participants [fasting reduces leptin; obese > lean]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting, only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle were found [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Found only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle despite abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting [no change]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Very high levels of β-hydroxybutyrate were found in the fasting muscle and a concomitant increase in β-hydroxybutyrate-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Found very high levels of β-OHB in fasting muscle and a concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle [fasting increases ketone bodies in muscle]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting [no change]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Myocellular energetics were preserved and there were no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Myocellular energetics were preserved during prolonged fasting with no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance [no change]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: In obese participants, the levels of the TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: In obese participants, levels of TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast [obese < lean]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Acetylcarnitine in obese were lower during 12 hours of fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72 hours of fasting [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Maltotriose, a metabolite of glycogenolysis, was reduced during prolonged fasting in lean only to levels comparable to obese [lean = obese]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting [fasting reduces glucose production]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12 hours of fasting [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12h of fasting [obese < lean]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed endogenous glucose production indicating that hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved [insulin effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed EGP indicating that hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved [insulin suppresses EGP; no difference between groups]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated net overnight glucose disposal during 12 hours fast, was reduced by approximately 45% [obese characteristic]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12h fast, was reduced by approximately 45% [obese < lean]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced approximately 20% in both groups [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced approximately 20% in both groups [fasting reduces glycogen]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Glycogen synthase phosphorylation increased during prolonged fasting in both lean and obese participants [fasting effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: GS phosphorylation increased during prolonged fasting in both lean and obese [fasting increases phosphorylation]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Insulin stimulation during 72 hours of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and glycogen synthase phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized [insulin effect]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Insulin stimulation during 72h of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized [insulin decreases glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Maltotriose, a metabolite of glycogenolysis, was reduced during prolonged fasting in lean only to levels comparable to obese [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: The metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting involving changes in glycogen breakdown are lost in muscle from insulin resistant obese participants [obese shows loss of adaptation]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: The metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting involving changes in glycogen breakdown are lost in muscle from insulin resistant obese participants [obese characteristic]
The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences
Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were approximately 2–3 fold higher in obese than lean [obese vs lean]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were approximately 2–3 fold higher in obese than lean [obese > lean]

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe 12-hour overnight fasting for control group; pass_1 adds 'overnight' descriptor
Both describe 72-hour fasting; pass_1 labels as 'Intervention', pass_2 as 'Prolonged'
Pass_1 contains total participant count (18 men); pass_2 is empty
Both report 9 lean participants; pass_1 specifies 'men', pass_2 omits gender
Pass_1 reports participant count (9 obese men); pass_2 reports BMI range (32-40 kg/m2) - different data types
Both report identical age inclusion criteria: 20 to 35 years
Both report identical BMI range for lean group: 19-23 kg/m2; minor label variation
Pass_1 contains BMI inclusion criteria for obese group (32-40 kg/m2); pass_2 is empty
Same inclusion criterion about low physical activity; only structural phrasing differs (Inclusion Criteria - Physical Activity vs Physical Activity Inclusion)
Same inclusion criterion for non-smokers; only structural phrasing differs (Inclusion Criteria - Smoking vs Smoking Status Inclusion)
Same inclusion criterion about no prescription medications; only structural phrasing differs (Inclusion Criteria - Medications vs Medication Inclusion)
Pass 1 specifies two timepoints (60 and 270 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one timepoint (60 minutes); these represent different sampling protocols
Same muscle biopsy sample size of approximately 100 mg; only minor label variation (Sample Size vs Size)
Identical information about HEC duration of 2 hours; exact match
Same insulin infusion rate of 30 mU/m2/min; only abbreviation expansion differs (HEC vs Hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp)
Same glucose target of ~5 mM during HEC; only label differs (Glucose Target Level vs Plasma Glucose Target) and abbreviation expansion
Pass 1 specifies two time intervals (180-210 AND 300-330 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one interval (180-210 minutes). These represent different measurement protocols.
Both describe the same assessment method (DEXA/dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry). Minor word order variation ('scan' vs 'absorptiometry' positioning) does not affect meaning.
Both describe HPLC method for HbA1c. Only difference is 'Analysis Method' vs 'Assay Method' terminology, which are synonymous in this context.
Both describe absorption photometry for ALAT. Only difference is 'Analysis Method' vs 'Assay Method' terminology.
Both describe aluminometric assay for TSH. Only difference is 'Analysis Method' vs 'Assay Method' terminology.
Both describe YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer. Pass 2 provides slightly more complete model designation in the third field, but both convey the same instrument.
Both describe ELISA for insulin analysis. Only difference is 'Analysis Method' vs 'Assay Method' terminology.
Both describe RIA for glucagon analysis. Only difference is 'Analysis Method' vs 'Assay Method' terminology.
Pass 1 contains metabolomics analysis method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains statistical significance level (P<0.05); Pass 2 is empty
Both describe randomized crossover design with 21-day separation; only difference is 'by minimum' vs 'by minimum of'
Pass 2 contains participant count for obese group (9); Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains muscle biopsy timing information (270 minutes); Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains indirect calorimetry timing information (300-330 minutes); Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains C-peptide assay method (ELISA); Pass 1 is empty
Pass 2 contains cortisol assay method (ELISA); Pass 1 is empty
Both describe the same finding: insulin decreased ≥50% during 72h fasting in both groups. Minor wording differences (72 hours vs 72h, different tag descriptions).
Both describe identical finding: insulin 15-30% higher in obese vs lean during hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp. Only abbreviation differences (HEC vs full name).
Both describe the same finding with identical numerical values (~35% lean, ~25% obese). Only tag notation differs (obese vs lean vs lean > obese).
Pass 1 discusses plasma glucose during HEC in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese vs lean. These are completely different metabolites and findings.
Both describe the same finding: insulin suppresses FFA in both groups, but effect is reduced during prolonged fasting. Minor wording and abbreviation differences (FFA vs free fatty acid).
Both describe identical finding: FFA 3-4 fold higher during HEC at 72h vs 12h fasting. Only abbreviation and notation differences (HEC vs hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp).
Both describe the same pattern: FFA higher in obese during overnight fast, lower during prolonged fasting. Minor wording and abbreviation differences.
Both describe the same finding: no increased FFA content in skeletal muscle from obese at any time point despite higher lipolytic rate during 12h fasting. Minor wording differences.
Both describe the same relationship: increased fat mass/obesity associated with higher leptin and lower adiponectin. Minor wording differences only.
Both state that prolonged fasting reduced leptin levels but levels remained higher in obese vs lean participants. Wording differs slightly but facts are identical.
Both describe modest increases in long-chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines despite abundant supply during fasting. Same fact, minor phrasing differences.
Both report high β-hydroxybutyrate levels in fasting muscle with concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine, demonstrating ketone body metabolism. β-OHB is abbreviation for β-hydroxybutyrate.
Both state no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD, AMP, ADP, ATP with prolonged fasting. Identical facts with negligible wording differences.

Both convey that myocellular energetics were preserved with no deficient substrate oxidation or mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance. Pass_2 adds 'during prolonged fasting' for clarity but same fact.
Both state TCA-cycle intermediates (citrate, succinate, malate) were reduced in obese compared to lean, independent of fasting duration. Same fact, negligible wording differences.

Pass_1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese during fasting. Pass_2 discusses maltotriose (glycogenolysis metabolite) in lean during fasting. These are completely different metabolites and different comparisons - not the same fact.
Both describe the same fact: endogenous glucose production decreases during prolonged fasting. Minor wording differences in the summary tags.
Both describe the same comparison: glucose production is lower in obese vs lean during 12 hours fasting. Only abbreviation and notation differences (12h vs 12 hours, < vs 'lower than').

Both describe insulin suppressing EGP and preserved hepatic sensitivity. Pass_2 adds 'no difference between groups' which is implicit in pass_1's 'in both' phrasing. Abbreviation differences (EGP vs endogenous glucose production).

Both describe the same finding: glycogenesis capacity in obese is reduced by ~45% measured via insulin-stimulated net overnight glucose disposal during 12h fast. Abbreviation and notation differences only (NOGD vs net overnight glucose disposal).
Both describe identical finding: skeletal muscle glycogen reduced ~20% during prolonged fasting in both groups. Only summary tag wording differs.
Both describe the same fact: glycogen synthase phosphorylation increases during prolonged fasting in both groups. Abbreviation (GS) and summary tag differences only.
